data.title: Reliance Jio IPO delayed due to regulatory limbo
data.description:
The Indian government’s delays in formalizing changes to listing rules are threatening to force Asia’s richest person, Mukesh Ambani, to miss the targeted timeline for an initial public offering of shares in Jio Platforms Ltd., the digital arm of his flagship company Reliance Industries Ltd.

Reliance is waiting for the government to formalize the changes backed by the regulator to appoint bankers formally and file a draft IPO prospectus, according to people familiar with the matter who asked not to be identified because the discussions are private. The company is now aiming to file the draft prospectus before April, depending on the government notification, they said.

Jio, which owns India’s largest wireless operator, is one of the crown jewels of Ambani’s empire and its IPO — the first listing of a major Reliance unit in almost 20 years — could be the country’s biggest ever. Investment bankers have proposed a valuation of as much as $170 billion for the company, which would offer a rare opportunity for investors to buy into one of world’s biggest growth stories of the past decade.

Mukesh Ambani said in August that Reliance was aiming to list Jio in the first half of 2026, a plan he had first signaled 2019 — back then with a five-year timeline. A top-end valuation could raise about $4.3 billion by selling the minimum stake and would place the company among the biggest companies in India by market value. Meta Platforms Inc. and Alphabet Inc. announced investments totaling more than $10 billion in Jio in 2020.

Deliberations are ongoing and details of the offering, including timing and size, may change, the people said. Reliance Industries declined to immediately comment. Representatives for the finance ministry didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment.

The Securities and Exchange Board of India in September approved amendments to its regulations, allowing companies with a post-issue market capitalization exceeding 5 trillion rupees ($55 billion) to dilute as little as 2.5% in an IPO, rather than the current minimum of 5%. The rule change is a possible catalyst for mega listings such as Jio and National Stock Exchange of India, but doesn’t yet have final government approval.

It’s unclear what the holdup is and there’s no indication that the delay is targeting the Jio IPO in particular.

The next step, which can usually take as long as a few months depending on government deliberations, is for the finance ministry to formally incorporate the changes and announce them in the Official Gazette, said Sonam Chandwani, managing partner at KS Legal & Associates.

“While the regulator has paved the way, the industry is now awaiting the final gazette notification, which we expect to see materialize in the first half of 2026,” said Ankita Singh, founder of Sarvaank Associates, a law firm.

NSE, meanwhile, is proceeding with plans to raise as much as $2.5 billion in an IPO. The company last month invited banks to pitch for roles in the offering. The two share sales would provide a much needed shot in the arm for the Indian market, where listings have struggled to start 2026 after two consecutive years of record fundraising.

data.title: Germany's Vincorion plans Frankfurt IPO to tap investor appetite for defence tech
data.description:
German defence and mission-critical power specialist Vincorion is preparing a stock market listing in Frankfurt within the first half of 2026, it said on Friday.

A former subsidiary of Jenoptik , Vincorion makes emergency power generators for the Eurofighter Typhoon and stabilisation systems that keep tank guns steady on the move, supplying NATO-linked defence programmes.

Its products are used in armoured vehicles and air defence systems such as IRIS-T and Patriot.

The company said its revenue reached revenue reached around 240 million euros in 2025, up from 200 million euros the previous year.

Its fixed order backlog stands at around 435 million euros, already reflecting a ramp-up of orders for generators and power electronics for land systems and air defence projects, and the company has been expanding capacity to meet demand.

data.title: Short-End U.S. Treasury Yields Decline, Focus Shifts to Employment Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0651 GMT - Short-dated U.S. Treasury yields decline in Asian trade, while medium- and longer-dated ones are little changed. Beside continued focus on the Middle East conflict, Friday's U.S. February employment data will take center stage at 1330 GMT. Analysts in a poll by The Wall Street Journal forecast job additions of 50,000 in February, below January's reading. The outcome is unlikely to change the Federal Reserve's pausing stance near term. "It seems difficult to envisage the jobs report materially moving the needle from a policy perspective, with the FOMC firmly in wait-and-see mode for now," says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. The two-year Treasury yield falls 1.8 bps to 3.580%; the 10-year Treasury yield is down 0.4 bps at 4.141%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0638 GMT - The two-year U.S. Treasury yield could rise to 3.80%-4.00% if the Middle East conflict escalates into a prolonged one, SEB's Jussi Hiljanen says in a note. The German two-year Schatz yield, meanwhile, could rise to 2.70%-2.80%, the chief rates strategist says. In the U.S., an initial flattening of the Treasury curve driven by a faster rise of short-end yields, could give way to a steeper curve driven by a faster rise in long-end yields, assuming that "markets do not move to price in a growth-collapse outcome," he says. The two-year U.S. Treasury yield last trades at 3.582% in Asian trade, while the two-year German Schatz yield finished at 2.221% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0635 GMT - The conflict in the Middle East has triggered a sharp bearish correction in German Bunds but that was "due anyway," Citi's Jamie Searle says in a note. Looking beyond near-term uncertainty, a broader range-bound view for Bunds remains intact, the rates strategist says. He expects the market to incline fading the geopolitical shock on the assumption that the macro implications of the conflict are more likely to be transitory than transformational. "The learnt experience of the last couple of decades feeds into this, with the global economy demonstrating resilience to a variety of shocks," Searle says. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.852% on Thursday, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0621 GMT - South Korea's inflation could be stronger than expected this year due to rising oil prices from Middle East tensions, ING's Min Joo Kang says. ING raises its forecast for Korea's 2026 consumer price index and expects it to rise 2.2% from its previous forecast for 2.0%. A sustained 10% rise in oil prices over one month is projected to lift CPI by about 0.4 percentage point, Kang says. She expects the government to cushion the inflationary shock with fuel-tax cuts, price caps on energy products and other policy tools. Taken together, those factors could add 0.2-0.4 percentage point to CPI in coming months, she adds. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0619 GMT - The U.S. labor market appears more stable than feared, Navellier & Associates' Louis Navellier says in a note. His comments come ahead of the release of February nonfarm payrolls data at 1330 GMT. The talk about all the information jobs at risk from AI replacement is being repeated regularly, Navellier says, That is good for corporate profits but not for consumer spending, he adds. Analysts in The Wall Street Journal's poll forecast the addition of 50,000 jobs in February, compared with 130,000 in January. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0616 GMT - Asia-Pacific utility companies should be able to absorb a short-lived disruption to fuel supplies due to the Iran conflict, Fitch Ratings says in a report. Fitch Ratings' credit ratings for companies are unlikely to change for now, as many benefit from strong government support, it says. Utilities in India, Indonesia and Australia are less affected by supply risks because most of their power-generation fuel--coal and natural gas--is sourced locally, and reliance on crude oil is negligible. China's gas utilities could see higher costs, but domestic pricing dynamics and procurement structures should cushion these expenses, the ratings company adds. Meanwhile, South Korea's utilities have the region's highest exposure to volatility in imported fuel prices. Credit pressure on companies could rise if the supply disruption becomes prolonged, Fitch Ratings says. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0604 GMT - A protracted conflict in the Middle East with higher and stickier energy prices and more persistent disruption to Gulf energy flows could push Brent towards or above $100 per barrel, prompting markets to reduce expectations of Federal Reserve rate cuts, SEB's Jussi Hiljanen says in a note. "Markets would continue fading Fed rate cut expectations, potentially fully wiping them out, which would lift short‑end bond yields significantly," the chief rates strategist says. While the U.S. is less energy‑vulnerable than Europe, higher global energy prices would still tighten financial conditions and increase the inflation risk premium, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Singapore's retail sales could rebound in February, given a low base, Selena Ling of OCBC Group Research says in a note. This comes as January's print unexpectedly fell 0.4% on year, partly skewed by the Lunar New Year, missing OCBC's forecast. For the year, the chief economist expects retail sales to grow around 2.0%-3.0%, similar to 2025. While the city-state's headline GDP growth is tipped to moderate to around 2.0%-4.0%, as officially projected, recent developments in the Middle East should be monitored closely for their potential duration and the severity of related disruptions, she adds. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0439 GMT - Australia is a big net energy exporter, but it is unlikely to gain much from the current surge in energy prices, says Abhijit Surya, economist at Capital Economics. This is largely because oil and gas companies aren't reinvesting their profits to boost domestic production, he says. And while the federal government will see higher tax revenue, the impetus to the economy would be modest, Surya adds. On the flip side, higher energy prices could significantly boost inflation this year, weighing heavily on households' purchasing power and the outlook for private consumption, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0439 GMT - Against a lower GDP target and moderate policy tone, China's economic stimulus this year is unlikely to lead to a prolonged market rerating as seen in 2H24, Daiwa analysts write in a note. Still, the analysts believe that the rollout of incremental measures between now and the Politburo meeting in mid-April can help offset geopolitical uncertainties, thus providing downside protection to economic activities and investor sentiment, they say. Apart from Daiwa's strategic positive view on China's technology, media and telecom sectors, it's still tactically positive on staples and real estate under stimulus tailwinds in the short term.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0431 GMT - While there seems to be no end in sight to the conflict in the Middle East, with U.S. President Trump indicating he is willing to fight for several more weeks, AMP expects him to eventually find a way to declare victory before significant energy disruption and higher inflation expectations become entrenched. My Bui, economist at AMP, says U.S. voters' top concern remains inflation and affordability, and with the midterm elections coming up, it is likely that Trump will take an off-ramp to limit consumer dissatisfaction with rising gasoline prices. There is also limited domestic support from the MAGA base and even the Republican Party for "forever wars," she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0416 GMT - A rate hike in Malaysia can't be ruled out if inflation proves stronger or more volatile than expected amid geopolitical uncertainties, particularly against a backdrop of robust economic prospects, RHB senior economist Chin Yee Sian says in a note. Strong growth and manageable inflation support a broadly stable policy outlook. However, escalating geopolitical tensions could push energy prices higher. Rising energy prices and potential subsidy reforms could lift inflation, although government support measures are likely to limit broader spillover effects, she reckons. RHB expects Bank Negara to keep the policy rate unchanged at 2.75% throughout 2026, with monetary policy remaining data-dependent at coming meetings and decisions guided by growth prospects and underlying inflation momentum. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Wizz Air Gets Tentative Approval to Operate U.K.-U.S. Flights
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

U.S. authorities tentatively approved a request by Wizz Air's U.K. subsidiary for a permit to operate flights between the U.K. and the U.S.

The U.S. Transportation Department cited the public interest as the grounds for its decision, but cautioned that it would consider any potential objections filed in the next 21 days to its announcement before granting final authorization.

The London-listed airline had applied for a foreign air carrier permit to operate flights between the two countries on Jan. 23.

If the Transportation Department confirms the permit, it will be effective for two years from the date of approval.

In 2022, Wizz Air's Hungary subsidiary failed to obtain a foreign air carrier permit to operate all-cargo flights to the U.S. Back then, the application met with opposition from several pilot unions, and was rejected.

Entering the U.S. market would offer new opportunities to the pan-European carrier, which recently stopped its Abu Dhabi-based operations citing engine problems caused by weather conditions and airspace closures in the Middle East due to regional conflicts. It also announced the closure of its Vienna base by this month citing unsustainable cost increases in recent years.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Fitch Upgrades CK Hutchison as Credit Concerns Ease
data.description:
By Jason Chau

CK Hutchison's progress in paring down debt earns it a credit rating upgrade from Fitch Ratings, which views the geopolitical risk facing the Hong Kong-listed conglomerate as manageable.

Fitch upgraded the company's credit rating to A from A- to reflect stable cash flow, continued deleveraging and a strong liquidity position.

Meanwhile, the cancellation of CK Hutchison's Panama Canal contract and the conflict in the Middle East are likely to have a limited direct impact, it said.

Earlier this year, Panama's Supreme Court voided CK Hutchison's contract to operate two ports at either end of the Panama Canal, allowing the government to seize the assets.

That cast uncertainty over the group's planned sale of most its port business, including the Panama ones, to a consortium that includes BlackRock.

Fitch placed CK Hutchison on ratings watch positive in March last year, when it announced the sale. However, progress has been slow amid geopolitical tensions, and the Panama developments raise further doubt, the ratings agency said.

It has removed CK Hutchison from the positive watch list, and sees the ratings outlook as stable.

Overall, the Panama ports' minimal contribution to group earnings and CK Hutchison's limited footprint in the Middle East suggest little financial impact, Fitch said. It estimates that ports in the region account for about 1% of total throughput.

Fitch expects steady earnings growth ahead for the company, and continued debt reduction from asset disposals, such as the proposed sale of U.K. Power Networks to French utility firm Engie.

Shares of CK Hutchison were last up 0.4% in Hong Kong.

Write to Jason Chau at jason.chau@wsj.com

data.title: Bnode sees broadly stable 2026 profit as Belgian mail volumes slide
data.description:
Bnode , recently rebranded from Bpost, said on Friday it expected its adjusted operating profit to land between 165 million and 195 million euros ($192 million and $226 million) in 2026, broadly in line with last year's number and market expectations.

Bnode's postal operations remain a drag on the business, with underlying domestic mail volumes falling around 11% in the fourth quarter. This decline was only partially offset by higher prices.

European peers are seeing a similar trend: Dutch postal operator PostNL flagged continuing erosion in letter volumes, while Britain's Royal Mail has argued that rules need to change as demand shrinks, reflecting the steady shift to digital communications.

  • Underlying parcel volumes grew 2.9% in the fourth quarter.

  • The 2026 outlook includes a roughly 35-million-euro drop in corporate EBIT, driven by salary indexation, higher headcount and increased business transformation-related costs.

  • 2025 adjusted EBIT at 179.7 million euros, slightly above a consensus of 177.3 million euros.

  • Growth in e-commerce logistics unit Staci and the Europe business region, helped by productivity measures, offset U.S. pressures.

  • Bnode's board will propose not to pay a dividend this year.

  • Q4 group operating income fell 7% from a year ago to 1.24 billion euros.

  • Q4 free cash flow slumped 25% to 144.5 million euros.

($1 = 0.8616 euros)

data.title: Hongkong Land Has Scope to Raise Dividends — Market Talk
data.description: Hongkong Land may have scope to raise dividends, DBS analysts say in a commentary. The Singapore-listed property company delivered a pleasant surprise with its full-year earnings, as its planned final dividend rose 12% on year to US$0.19 a share, beating DBS's estimates. The company is in a strong financial position and its real-estate fund is likely to enable more capital-recycling efforts, the analysts say. Hongkong Land could therefore pursue prime investment opportunities and consider higher dividends, they add. Its near-term share price could be boosted by continuing share buybacks, too. DBS raises its target price to US$10.17 from US$10.13 and maintains a buy rating. Shares rise 2.7% to US$8.37. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)
